title: BECALMED
byline: Generation ship 'Long Patience'. Reactor cold. No one left but you.

character.backstory: Eighteen years on the watch, the last six standing the reactor vigil on the 'Long Patience' - the one post where you never sleep, because the manual key is an arm's length away and the core does not wait. You know exactly how cold a reactor has to get before it cannot come back, and exactly how long that took. You were asleep for all of it.

watching: THE REACTOR WENT COLD ON YOUR WATCH.
YOU SLEPT THROUGH ALL OF IT.
THEY STOPPED TRYING TO WAKE YOU.
THE COLD IS ALMOST DONE NOW.
THE KEY WAS AN ARM AWAY.
YOUR NAME IS AT THE TOP.
YOU LET THE LONG PATIENCE END.

watchroom.title: Watch station
watchroom.text:
You wake in the chair you were not supposed to sleep in. One backup cell throws a slow green across the dead consoles, and the green is failing. No drive-hum under your feet. No air-handler. The ship that has carried four generations toward a star none of you will live to see has stopped.

The shift log is open. Your name is at the top of it. The reactor went cold on your watch. The timestamp says nine days ago.

choices.text: Read down the log past your name.
Stand up. Go find the others.

watchroom_log.title: Watch station
watchroom_log.text: You scroll. The entries after yours are not entries. Four words, logged by hand, once an hour, by crew who were awake when you were not: he will not wake. Then again. Then a column of it, down and down, in different hands, until the hands stop.

choices.text: Close the log. Go find them.

spine.title: Spinal corridor
spine.text:
The corridor runs the ship's whole length. Frost has started on the rivets, fine as mold. Your breath hangs in front of you and does not move. The air is becalmed - going nowhere, with you inside it.

Forward, the bridge, where the watch was kept. Aft, the muster hall, where the others would have gathered. Amidships, the lifeboat lock.

sanityText.45: Your breath hangs where you left it, marking each place you have already stood. The frost has climbed the walls in patterns that keep almost spelling the same four words: he will not wake. Forward: the bridge. Aft: the muster hall. Amidships: the lifeboat lock.

choices.text: Forward, to the bridge.
Aft, to the muster hall.
Amidships, to the lifeboat lock.
Sit down here in the cold and stop.

bridge.title: Bridge
bridge.text:
Every console is dark. The forward port shows the same handful of stars it has shown for forty years, none of them any nearer. The captain's seat faces the dark, and the captain is in it, hands folded. He stayed when the others went to the boat. The cold kept him where he chose to sit.

The manual reactor key kept its place on the helm through all of it - the key the watch turns the instant the core falters, the key that was an arm's length from your chair while you slept.

choices.text: Take the reactor key from the helm.
Stand at the captain's shoulder and look at him.
Carry the key down to the reactor deck. You will not come back up.
Back to the corridor.

bridge_seat.title: Bridge
bridge_seat.text:
You come around the seat and stand at the captain's shoulder, where the watch reports were given. The cold has kept him exact: head up, eyes open under a film of frost, fixed on the forward port.

He kept the watch you left. All the way out.

choices.text: Step back.

muster.title: Muster hall
muster.text:
The long tables are set. The meal on them is frozen solid, every plate full, as though everyone rose at once and went somewhere better together. No bodies. Only the places they kept, and the cold keeping them.

A wall locker hangs open, stripped near to bare. Someone left one med-gel ampoule, the field kind - for you, or by accident; you will not know which. Down the tables, one place is set apart, the chair pulled out. The watch-keeper's. Yours.

choices.text: Take the med-gel ampoule.
Walk down the tables to your own pulled-out chair.
Back to the corridor.

muster_chair.title: Muster hall
muster_chair.text:
You walk the length of the tables to the pulled-out chair. The place is set like all the others, the plate full and furred with frost - but here the cup is poured as well, and the chair is turned a little off true, toward the hatch.

They kept it facing the door.

choices.text: Step away from the chair.

lock.title: Lifeboat lock
lock.text:
The cradle is empty. The one boat the ship carried, the one mercy for four hundred, is gone - launched days ago by hands that did the arithmetic and counted you among the lost.

The outer hatch is still here. Manual, a wheel a child could turn, onto the same dark they chose. Frost has reached the spokes. Your hand fits them exactly.

choices.text: Press your face to the inner port and look for where the boat went.
Turn the wheel. Follow them out.
Back to the corridor.

lock_port.title: Lifeboat lock
lock_port.text:
Your breath freezes to the port at once. You scrape a circle clear with your thumb and look out along the launch line, into the dark they went into.

The boat is still there. A boat-shaped black against the stars, holding off the hull at the distance of a thrown stone. Drive dark. Ports dark. Days now, and it has not gone anywhere either.

choices.text: Step back from the port.

reactor.title: Reactor deck
reactor.text: The core is a black slab gone to absolute cold. The key fits the manual socket the way it would have nine days ago, if you had been awake. You turn it now. Nothing catches. The fuel is past restart, the moderators frozen solid through - you know to the hour when that became true, and the hour was the first one. The key turns and turns in a dead lock, and its small click is the only sound the ship has left.

choices.text: Keep turning the key until your fingers stop.

ending.text: You turn the key until your fingers will not close, then stand there meaning to turn it. The cold takes you on your feet, the way it took the ship - slowly, from the outside in. Far forward, the last green cell gives out, and the ship keeps its long patience with you at last.

The wheel gives easily. The hatch breaks its seal, and the dark takes the air in one soft pull, and then takes you. For one clean moment there is no cold at all. They went out in the one boat, with air and arithmetic. You go out as you are.

You sit down under the frost where it spells he will not wake, and you do not get up. There is nowhere the getting up would take you. You keep the ship's last watch the way you kept the first one - sitting down - and the frost climbs from the rivets onto your sleeves, fine as mold, in no hurry at all.
